response:
D

The question concerns which substance absorbs the most incoming solar energy (low albedo) while also storing the largest quantity of it (high specific heat capacity). Snow has very high albedo (~80-90% reflectivity), so it absorbs and retains the least energy overall. Sand and grass have moderate-to-high absorption (albedo ~15-30%) but low specific heats (~0.8 J/g°C for sand; similar for organic grass), so they heat rapidly with limited total energy storage per degree of temperature change. Water has the lowest albedo of the options (~5-10%, with much of the radiation penetrating and being absorbed below the surface) combined with the highest specific heat (4.18 J/g°C), enabling it to take in and store far more total solar energy without a large temperature rise. This combination makes water the clear choice for retaining the largest amount.
